Kinley speaking Dzongkha (Wikitongues)
Jakar Dzong, representative of the distinct dzong architecture from which Dzongkha gets its name
Dzongkha[a] (རྫོང་ཁ་ [d͡zòŋkʰɑ́]), also known by its exonym Bhutanese, is a Tibeto-Burman language in the Tibetic language family that is primarily spoken by the Bhutanese people. It is the official and national language of Bhutan, and is written using the Tibetan script in formal contexts, with informal correspondence in the Joyig script.[5]

The word dzongkha means "the language of the fortress", from dzong "fortress" and kha "language". As of 2013[update], Dzongkha had 171,080 native speakers and about 640,000 total speakers.[2]

Dzongkha is a South Tibetic language. It is closely related to Laya and Lunana and partially intelligible with Sikkimese, and to some other Bhutanese languages such as Chochangachakha, Brokpa, Brokkat and Lakha. It has a more distant relationship to Standard Tibetan.
examples: Bhutanese Tibetanརྫོང་ཁ་"Dzongkha" written in the Joyig script (top) and the Uchen script (bottom)Pronunciation[d͡zòŋkʰɑ́]Native toBhutanEthnicityNgalop peopleNative speakers171,080 (2013)[1]
The word dzongkha means "the language of the fortress", from dzong "fortress" and kha "language". As of 2013[update], Dzongkha had 171,080 native speakers and about 640,000 total speakers.[2]

chapters.title: Classification
chapters.summary: Dzongkha is considered a South Tibetic language. It is closely related to and partially intelligible with Sikkimese, and to some other Bhutanese languages such as Chochangachakha, Brokpa, Brokkat and Lakha.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
Dzongkha is considered a South Tibetic language. It is closely related to and partially intelligible with Sikkimese, and to some other Bhutanese languages such as Chochangachakha, Brokpa, Brokkat and Lakha.

Dzongkha bears a close linguistic relationship to J'umowa, which is spoken in the Chumbi Valley of Southern Tibet.[6] It has a much more distant relationship to Standard Tibetan. Spoken Dzongkha and Tibetan are around 50% to 80% mutually intelligible, with the literary forms of both highly influenced by the liturgical (clerical) Classical Tibetan language, known in Bhutan as Chöke (ཆོས་སྐད།), which has been used for centuries by Buddhist monks. Chöke was used as the language of education in Bhutan until the early 1960s when it was replaced by Dzongkha in public schools.[7]

Although descended from Classical Tibetan, Dzongkha shows a great many irregularities in sound changes that make the official spelling and standard pronunciation more distant from each other than is the case with Standard Tibetan. It has been written that "traditional orthography and modern phonology are two distinct systems operating by a distinct set of rules" in Dzongkha.[8]
examples: Although descended from Classical Tibetan, Dzongkha shows a great many irregularities in sound changes that make the official spelling and standard pronunciation more distant from each other than is the case with Standard Tibetan. It has be

chapters.title: Usage
chapters.summary: Dzongkha and its dialects are the native tongue of eight western districts of Bhutan (viz. Wangdue Phodrang, Punakha, Thimphu, Gasa, Paro, Ha, Dagana and Chukha).[9] There are also some native speakers near the Indian town of Kalimpong, once part of Bhutan but now in North Bengal

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
Dzongkha and its dialects are the native tongue of eight western districts of Bhutan (viz. Wangdue Phodrang, Punakha, Thimphu, Gasa, Paro, Ha, Dagana and Chukha).[9] There are also some native speakers near the Indian town of Kalimpong, once part of Bhutan but now in North Bengal, and in Sikkim.

Dzongkha was declared the national language of Bhutan in 1971.[10] Dzongkha study is mandatory in all schools, and the language is the lingua franca in the districts to the south and east where it is not the mother tongue. The Bhutanese films Travellers and Magicians (2003) and Lunana: A Yak in the Classroom (2019) are in Dzongkha.

chapters.title: Phonology
chapters.summary: Dzongkha is a tonal language and has two register tones: high and low.[11] The tone of a syllable determines the allophone of the onset and the phonation type of the nuclear vowel.[12]

sections.heading: Tones
sections.body: Dzongkha is a tonal language and has two register tones: high and low.[11] The tone of a syllable determines the allophone of the onset and the phonation type of the nuclear vowel.[12]

sections.heading: Consonants

All consonants may begin a syllable. In the onsets of low-tone syllables, consonants are voiced.[12] Aspirated consonants (indicated by the superscript h), /ɬ/, and /h/ are not found in low-tone syllables.[12] The rhotic /r/ is usually a trill [r] or a fricative trill [r̝],[11] and is voiceless in the onsets of high-tone syllables.[12]

/t, tʰ, ts, tsʰ, s/ are dental.[11] Descriptions of the palatal affricates and fricatives vary from alveolo-palatal to plain palatal.[11][13][12]

Only a few consonants are found in syllable-final positions. Most common among them are /m, n, p/.[12] Syllable-final /ŋ/ is often elided and results in the preceding vowel nasalized and prolonged, especially word-finally.[14][12] Syllable-final /k/ is most often omitted when word-final as well, unless in formal speech.[12] In literary pronunciation, liquids /r/ and /l/ may also end a syllable.[11] Though rare, /ɕ/ is also found in syllable-final positions.[11][12] No other consonants are found in syllable-final positions.

sections.heading: Vowels
sections.body:
Vowel phonemes

Front

Back

Close

i  iː  yː

u  uː

Mid

e  eː  øː

o  oː

Open

ɛː

ɑ  ɑː

When in low tone, vowels are produced with breathy voice.[11][14]

In closed syllables, /i/ varies between [i] and [ɪ], the latter being more common.[11][12]

/yː/ varies between [yː] and [ʏː].[11]

/e/ varies between close-mid [e] and open-mid [ɛ], the latter being common in closed syllables. /eː/ is close-mid [eː]. /eː/ may not be longer than /e/ at all, and differs from /e/ more often in quality than in length.[11]

Descriptions of /øː/ vary between close-mid [øː] and open-mid [œː].[11][12]

/o/ is close-mid [o], but may approach open-mid [ɔ] especially in closed syllables. /oː/ is close-mid [oː].[11]

/ɛː/ is slightly lower than open-mid, i.e. [ɛ̞ː].[11]

/ɑ/ may approach [ɐ], especially in closed syllables.[11][12]

When nasalized or followed by [ŋ], vowels are always long.[14][12]

sections.heading: Phonotactics
sections.body: Many words in Dzongkha are monosyllabic.[12] Syllables usually take the form of CVC, CV, or VC.[12] Syllables with complex onsets are also found, but such an onset must be a combination of an unaspirated bilabial stop and a palatal affricate.[12] The bilabial stops in complex onsets are often omitted in colloquial speech.[12]

chapters.title: Writing system
chapters.summary:
Main articles: Joyig script, Tibetan script, Roman Dzongkha, Dzongkha numerals, and Dzongkha Braille
The Joyig alphabet script
The Tibetan script used to write Dzongkha has thirty basic letters, sometimes known as "radicals", for consonants. Dzongkha is usually written in Bhutane

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
Main articles: Joyig script, Tibetan script, Roman Dzongkha, Dzongkha numerals, and Dzongkha Braille
The Joyig alphabet script
The Tibetan script used to write Dzongkha has thirty basic letters, sometimes known as "radicals", for consonants. Dzongkha is usually written in Bhutanese forms of the Uchen script, forms of the Tibetan script known as Jôyi "cursive longhand" and Jôtshum "formal longhand". The print form is known simply as Tshûm.[15]
examples: The Tibetan script used to write Dzongkha has thirty basic letters, sometimes known as "radicals", for consonants. Dzongkha is usually written in Bhutanese forms of the Uchen script, forms of the Tibetan script known as Jôyi "cursive longha

sections.heading: Romanization
sections.body: There are various systems of romanization and transliteration for Dzongkha, but none accurately represents its phonetic sound.[16] The Bhutanese government adopted a transcription system known as Roman Dzongkha, devised by the linguist George van Driem, as its standard in 1991.[10]

chapters.title: Grammar
chapters.summary:
Number
Dzongkha nouns distinguish between singular (unmarked) and plural, with the plural either unmarked or suffixed with ཚུ་ -tshu. The use of the plural suffix is not obligatory and is used mainly for emphasis.[17][18]

sections.heading: Nouns
sections.body:
Number
Dzongkha nouns distinguish between singular (unmarked) and plural, with the plural either unmarked or suffixed with ཚུ་ -tshu. The use of the plural suffix is not obligatory and is used mainly for emphasis.[17][18]

Case
Dzongkha nouns are marked for 5 cases: genitive, locative, ablative, dative and ergative.[19]

genitive case: marks possession and is often translated as "of". There are 4 genitive suffixes in written Dzongkha:
གྱི་ -g°i - after words ending in མ་, ན་, ར་, ལ་.

གི་ -g°i - after words ending in ག་, ང་ and certain words ending in a vowel.

ཀྱི་ -g°i - after words ending in བ་, ད་, ས་.

འི་ -i after certain words ending in a vowel.

locative case - marks location or destination and is often translated as "in", "at" or "on". It's indicated by the suffix ནང་ -na.

ablative case - marks direction away from the noun and is often translated as "from". It's indicated by the suffix ལས་ -lä.

dative case - marks the goal or where an activity takes place and is often translated as "to", "for" or "at". It's indicated by the suffix ལུ་ -lu.

ergative case - used for ergative and instrumental functions. There are 3 ergative suffixes in written Dzongkha:
གྱིས་ -g°i - after words ending in མ་, ན་, ར་, ལ་.

གིས་ -g°i - after words ending in ག་, ང་ or a vowel.

ཀྱིས་ -g°i - after words ending in བ་, ད་, ས་.

Derivation
As in other Tibetic languages, compounding is the most common method for deriving new nouns in Dzongkha. A compound usually consists of two (or, less commonly, more) monossyllabic roots, which can be either free or bound.[20]

Root 1
Root 2
Compound noun
Notes

བསྟོད​་ tö (praise)
ར་ ra
བསྟོད​་ར་ töra (praise)
ར་ ra is a bound morpheme with no meaning of its own.

ཁབ་ khap (cover)
ཏོག་ to (top)
ཁབ་ཏོག་ khapto (lid)
ཏོག་ to is a bound morpheme and means something like "top" in most (though not all) compounds.

རྡོ་ do (stone)
གནག་ nak (black)
རྡོ་གནག་ donak (graphite)
examples: genitive case: marks possession and is often translated as "of". There are 4 genitive suffixes in written Dzongkha:
locative case - marks location or destination and is often translated as "in", "at" or "on". It's indicated by the suffix ནང་ -na.
ablative case - marks direction away from the noun and is often translated as "from". It's indicated by the suffix ལས་ -lä.
dative case - marks the goal or where an activity takes place and is often translated as "to", "for" or "at". It's indicated by the suffix ལུ་ -lu.
ཏོག་ to is a bound morpheme and means something like "top" in most (though not all) compounds.

ནཱ་བུ་ nâb°u (they; you all)

The honorific pronoun ནཱ༌ nâ and its plural form are used when one wants to show respect to the person being addressed or to a 3rd person of either gender.

sections.heading: Verbs
sections.body:
This section needs expansion. You can help by adding missing information.  (July 2024)
Dzongkha verbs inflect for tense, aspect and mood, but unlike many languages, they do not show agreement for person and number.[21]

Conjugation
Dzongkha verbs are mainly marked for the progressive (དོ་ -do), the witnessed past (ཡི་ -yi; ཅི་ -ci), the inferred past (ནུག་ -nu), the perfective (སོ་ so), the present continuous (པའི་སྒང་ ‑bigang ~ ‑migang; བའི་སྒང་ ‑wigang), the adhortative (གེ་ ‑ge), the optative (བཅུག་ cu), and the supine (པར་ ‑ba ~ -ma; བར་ ‑wa). They are also marked for things like the steady state  present, "acquired knowledge", factual tenses, along many other modals.[22]

Copula
In Dzongkha, there are 5 copular verbs that can be translated as "to be" in English: ཨིན་ 'ing, ཨིན་པས་ 'immä, ཡོད་ yö, འདུག་ du and སྨོ་ 'mo.
examples: Dzongkha verbs are mainly marked for the progressive (དོ་ -do), the witnessed past (ཡི་ -yi; ཅི་ -ci), the inferred past (ནུག་ -nu), the perfective (སོ་ so), the present continuous (པའི་སྒང་ ‑bigang ~ ‑migang; བའི་སྒང་ ‑wigang), the adhorta
In Dzongkha, there are 5 copular verbs that can be translated as "to be" in English: ཨིན་ 'ing, ཨིན་པས་ 'immä, ཡོད་ yö, འདུག་ du and སྨོ་ 'mo.

sections.heading: Adjectives
sections.body:
Comparison
The comparative is indicated by the suffix བ་ -wa  ("than") while the superlative is indicated by the suffix ཤོས་ -sho ("the most", "-est").[23]
examples: The comparative is indicated by the suffix བ་ -wa  ("than") while the superlative is indicated by the suffix ཤོས་ -sho ("the most", "-est").[23]

’Gro- ba- mi- rigs- ga- ra- dbaṅ- cha- ’dra- mtam- ’bad- sgyew- las- ga- ra- gis- gcig- lu- spun- cha’i- dam- tshig- bstan- dgo

All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.[25]
